enrichment.short_description: Desktop SEO crawler software used by SEO professionals to audit websites for technical issues, broken links, redirects, and duplicate content.
enrichment.pricing_model: Freemium (free version plus paid annual per-user license)
enrichment.starting_price: 199 GBP per year per license (about 279 USD)
enrichment.seo_title: Screaming Frog SEO Spider Review 2026: Pricing and Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: See what Screaming Frog SEO Spider costs, its crawl features, and whether the desktop SEO crawler fits your technical audit workflow in 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Screaming Frog SEO Spider is a desktop website crawler built by Screaming Frog, a UK search marketing agency founded in 2010 and headquartered in Henley-on-Thames, Oxfordshire, England, with a team of roughly 60 people. The SEO Spider tool crawls websites the way search engine bots do, fetching URLs, links, images, CSS, and scripts to surface technical SEO issues such as broken links, server errors, redirect chains, duplicate content, missing metadata, malformed structured data, and orphaned pages. It runs natively on Windows, macOS, and Ubuntu, and stores crawl data in a local database or in memory so users can pause, save, and resume large crawls. The software ships in two tiers: a free version capped at crawling 500 URLs per crawl with a reduced feature set, and a paid version priced at 199 GBP per year per license (about 279 USD or 245 EUR), which removes the URL cap, subject to available memory and storage, and unlocks JavaScript rendering, Google Analytics and Search Console integration, custom extraction via XPath, CSS Path, or regex, crawl and directory tree visualizations, scheduling, and API access for tools like PageSpeed Insights and Moz. Screaming Frog offers volume discounts starting at five licenses, roughly 5 percent off, up to 20 or more licenses, roughly 15 percent off, and licenses are sold per user and renew annually. The tool is widely used by in-house SEO teams, agencies, and consultants for site audits, log file analysis alongside the companion Log File Analyser product, and migration quality assurance. It integrates with Google Search Console, Google Analytics 4, the PageSpeed Insights API, and major rank-tracking and backlink tools. Because it is a locally installed desktop application rather than a cloud SaaS platform, crawl data stays on the user's machine, which appeals to agencies handling sensitive client sites. The license model has remained a flat annual per-seat fee for years, distinguishing it from subscription-based SaaS crawlers such as Sitebulb, Ahrefs Site Audit, or Semrush Site Audit that charge based on crawl volume or bundle broader SEO suites. Screaming Frog remains one of the most widely referenced tools in SEO training material and certification courses worldwide.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Screaming Frog SEO Spider is a Windows, macOS, and Ubuntu desktop application that crawls websites like a search engine bot to uncover technical SEO problems. Built by the UK agency Screaming Frog, founded in 2010, it has become one of the most widely used tools in the SEO industry for site audits and migrations.
The tool is locally installed rather than cloud-based, meaning crawl data is stored on the user's own machine unless exported, which many agencies value for handling client data privately.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The SEO Spider surfaces broken links, redirect chains, duplicate titles and meta descriptions, missing alt text, malformed structured data, and orphaned pages. It supports JavaScript rendering for crawling modern web apps, custom extraction using XPath, CSS Path, or regular expressions, and integrates directly with Google Analytics, Google Search Console, and the PageSpeed Insights API to pull performance and traffic data into the same crawl report.

Additional capabilities include crawl and directory-tree visualizations, XML sitemap generation, scheduled crawling, and a command-line interface for automation, making it suitable for both one-off audits and recurring monitoring.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The free version crawls up to 500 URLs and includes most core features. The paid license costs 199 GBP per year per user and removes the crawl limit, subject to available memory and disk space, while unlocking JavaScript rendering, API integrations, and scheduling. Volume discounts apply starting at 5 licenses and scale up to 20 or more licenses purchased together.

features.title: Broken link and redirect detection
features.description: Identifies 4xx and 5xx errors, redirect chains, and redirect loops across an entire crawled site.

features.title: Custom extraction
features.description: Pulls specific page data using XPath, CSS Path, or regex to scrape structured content during a crawl.

features.title: JavaScript rendering
features.description: Crawls JavaScript-heavy sites and single-page apps by rendering pages before analysis.

features.title: Third-party integrations
features.description: Connects to Google Analytics, Search Console, and PageSpeed Insights to combine crawl and performance data.

features.title: Visualizations
features.description: Generates crawl diagrams and directory tree maps to visualize site structure and internal linking.

features.title: Duplicate content auditing
features.description: Flags duplicate titles, meta descriptions, and near-duplicate page content across the site.

features.title: XML sitemap generation
features.description: Builds XML sitemaps directly from crawl data for submission to search engines.

features.title: Scheduling and automation
features.description: Runs crawls on a schedule and supports command-line operation for automated workflows.

pros: Extremely detailed crawl data and granular control over crawl settings
Flat annual per-seat fee rather than consumption-based pricing
Runs locally, keeping crawl data off third-party servers
Industry-standard tool with extensive documentation and community knowledge
Highly configurable to mimic different bots and rendering modes
cons: Desktop-only, with no native cloud or SaaS dashboard
Steep learning curve for SEO beginners
Can be memory-intensive on very large crawls without database storage mode
Free version limited to 500 URLs per crawl
No built-in rank tracking or backlink index, requiring separate tools

faqs.question: Is Screaming Frog SEO Spider free?
faqs.answer: Yes, a free version is available that crawls up to 500 URLs with most core features. Crawling beyond that limit requires a paid license.

faqs.question: How much does a Screaming Frog license cost?
faqs.answer: A single license costs 199 GBP per year (about 279 USD), billed annually per user, with volume discounts for 5 or more licenses.

faqs.question: Does Screaming Frog work on Mac and Linux?
faqs.answer: Yes, it runs natively on Windows, macOS, and Ubuntu.

faqs.question: Can Screaming Frog crawl JavaScript websites?
faqs.answer: Yes, the paid version supports JavaScript rendering to crawl single-page applications and dynamically loaded content.

faqs.question: Does Screaming Frog integrate with Google Analytics and Search Console?
faqs.answer: Yes, both integrations are available in the paid version, letting users combine crawl data with traffic and search performance metrics.

faqs.question: Is Screaming Frog a cloud-based tool?
faqs.answer: No, it is a locally installed desktop application; crawl data stays on the user's machine unless manually exported.

faqs.question: Does Screaming Frog offer log file analysis?
faqs.answer: Log file analysis is handled by a separate companion product, the Screaming Frog Log File Analyser, sold independently.

faqs.question: Are licenses shared across multiple users?
faqs.answer: No, licenses are sold per user; each additional user requires its own license, though volume discounts apply.

use_cases.title: Technical SEO audits
use_cases.description: Consultants and agencies use the SEO Spider to identify broken links, redirect issues, duplicate content, and metadata problems across client websites.

use_cases.title: Site migration quality assurance
use_cases.description: Teams crawl staging and live sites before and after a migration to compare URL structures, redirects, and indexability.

use_cases.title: Ongoing site health monitoring
use_cases.description: Scheduled crawls track new technical issues over time as sites are updated, feeding into regular SEO reporting.

features: Most core crawl and audit features
No JavaScript rendering or API integrations
Unlimited URL crawling, subject to memory and storage
Google Analytics and Search Console integration
Custom extraction, scheduling, and API access
Volume discount of roughly 15 percent per license

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source, self-hosted screen-sharing tool for developers, using WebRTC for low-latency, browser-based screen sharing with no client install.
enrichment.seo_title: Screego Review 2026: Free Self-Hosted Screen Sharing for Developers
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Screego is a free, open-source, self-hosted WebRTC screen-sharing tool for developers. See its features, setup, and how it compares to paid alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Screego is a free and open-source, self-hosted screen-sharing application built specifically for developers who want a fast, low-latency alternative to sharing screens through corporate chat tools like Slack, Microsoft Teams, or Zoom. The project, hosted at github.com/screego/server and licensed under the GNU General Public License v3.0, is written primarily in Go and has accumulated more than 10,000 stars on GitHub, reflecting strong adoption in the self-hosted and homelab communities. Screego runs entirely inside the browser using WebRTC, meaning neither the person sharing their screen nor the viewers need to install client software beyond a modern web browser. Because WebRTC mandates DTLS and SRTP encryption for all media streams, the Screego server itself only relays encrypted packets between peers and cannot decrypt or view the shared screen content, which makes it attractive for privacy-conscious teams and individuals. The server includes a built-in TURN server for NAT traversal, so self-hosters do not need to separately stand up and configure a TURN relay to make peer-to-peer connections work behind firewalls and NATs. Deployment is designed to be simple: Screego ships as a single compiled binary or an official Docker image, and the project states it can be running in under two minutes using Docker. Typical use cases include ad hoc pair programming and code review sessions, remote debugging, small team screen-sharing rooms for distributed engineering teams, and privacy-sensitive organizations that want to avoid routing screen-share traffic through third-party SaaS video platforms. Screego supports multiple simultaneous viewers per shared session and room-based joining via short room codes or links, and it works across desktop and mobile browsers without app installs. Because it is a self-hosted open-source project rather than a company-backed product, there is no formal pricing, sales team, SLA, or enterprise support tier; support instead comes through the GitHub repository's issues and discussions, and a public demo instance is available at app.screego.net for trying the tool before self-hosting it. Screego is best understood as a lightweight, single-purpose alternative to full-featured video conferencing suites: it deliberately omits chat, recording, whiteboarding, and calendar integration, focusing solely on doing screen sharing well with minimal latency and maximum self-hosting control for technical teams that already run their own infrastructure.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Screego is an open-source, self-hosted screen-sharing tool aimed at developers who want a lightweight, low-latency alternative to bundled screen sharing in tools like Zoom or Teams. It runs in the browser using WebRTC and requires no client installation for viewers.
Because it is community-maintained under the GPL-3.0 license rather than sold by a company, Screego has no pricing tiers, sales contact, or commercial support plan.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Screego provides browser-based screen sharing with an integrated TURN server for NAT traversal, so self-hosters do not need to configure separate relay infrastructure. All media streams are encrypted end to end via WebRTC's DTLS/SRTP requirements, and the server cannot view shared screen content.

The tool supports multiple simultaneous viewers per room, short shareable room codes, and deployment via a single binary or Docker image, making it fast to stand up on a home server, VPS, or internal company infrastructure.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Screego itself is entirely free and open source under the GPL-3.0 license. The only cost is whatever infrastructure the user chooses to self-host it on, such as a small VPS or existing home server; there are no subscription tiers, seat licenses, or paid upgrades from the project itself.

features.title: Browser-based WebRTC screen sharing
features.description: Viewers join through a link and watch in-browser with no software installation required.

features.title: Built-in TURN server
features.description: Includes an integrated TURN server for NAT traversal, simplifying self-hosted deployment behind firewalls.

features.title: End-to-end encrypted streams
features.description: WebRTC's DTLS/SRTP requirements mean the server only relays encrypted media it cannot decrypt.

features.title: Single binary or Docker deployment
features.description: Deploys as one compiled binary or an official Docker image, runnable in minutes.

features.title: Multi-viewer rooms
features.description: Supports multiple simultaneous viewers per screen-share session via shareable room codes.

features.title: Low-latency streaming
features.description: Peer-to-peer WebRTC connections minimize delay compared to relayed video conferencing tools.

features.title: Open-source GPL-3.0 license
features.description: Fully open source, self-hostable, and free to modify or audit.

features.title: Cross-platform browser support
features.description: Works across desktop and mobile browsers without dedicated native apps.

pros: Completely free and open source
Full data control through self-hosting
No client software installation required for viewers
Low latency thanks to peer-to-peer WebRTC
Built-in TURN server simplifies deployment
Lightweight and focused on a single purpose
cons: Requires self-hosting know-how such as server, domain, and TLS setup
No official commercial support or SLA
Lacks recording, chat, or whiteboard features
No hosted or managed version offered by the project itself
Limited to screen sharing rather than a full conferencing suite

faqs.question: Is Screego really free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Screego is free and open source under the GPL-3.0 license; the only cost is the infrastructure you self-host it on.

faqs.question: Do viewers need to install anything to watch a Screego share?
faqs.answer: No, viewers join through a browser link and watch entirely in-browser using WebRTC, with no app installation required.

faqs.question: Can Screego be used without self-hosting?
faqs.answer: There is a public demo instance at app.screego.net for trying it, but production use is intended to be self-hosted on your own server.

faqs.question: Is Screego secure?
faqs.answer: Media streams are encrypted end to end via WebRTC's DTLS/SRTP protocols, and the Screego server only relays encrypted packets without being able to view the content.

faqs.question: Does Screego support recording sessions?
faqs.answer: No, Screego is focused solely on live screen sharing and does not include built-in recording, chat, or whiteboard features.

faqs.question: How is Screego deployed?
faqs.answer: It can be deployed as a single compiled binary or via the official Docker image, typically running within a couple of minutes.

faqs.question: What license does Screego use?
faqs.answer: Screego is released under the GNU General Public License version 3.0 (GPL-3.0).

faqs.question: Who maintains Screego?
faqs.answer: Screego is an open-source community project hosted on GitHub at screego/server, maintained by volunteer contributors rather than a company.

use_cases.title: Remote pair programming and code review
use_cases.description: Developers share their screen directly in-browser for live coding sessions without routing through a corporate video tool.

use_cases.title: Privacy-conscious internal screen sharing
use_cases.description: Organizations that want full control over data and infrastructure self-host Screego instead of using third-party SaaS conferencing tools.

use_cases.title: Lightweight team screen-share rooms
use_cases.description: Distributed teams stand up a persistent internal Screego instance for quick ad hoc screen-sharing without scheduling a full meeting.

features: Full source code access under GPL-3.0

enrichment.short_description: macOS screen recording app that automatically applies professional zoom, cursor effects, and polish to produce demo-ready videos without manual editing.
enrichment.pricing_model: Subscription (monthly or annual), free to record and edit but export requires a paid plan
enrichment.starting_price: 9 USD per month billed annually (108 USD per year)
enrichment.seo_title: Screen Studio Review 2026: Pricing and Features for Mac Screen Recording
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Screen Studio automates polished screen recordings on Mac. See its pricing plans, features, and who the indie-built app is best suited for in 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Screen Studio is a macOS-only screen recording application created by Polish indie developer Adam Pietrasiak, who launched the product in late 2022 alongside co-founders Maciej and David; the small, bootstrapped team is based in Krakow, Poland, and has grown the product without outside venture funding to thousands of paying customers. Screen Studio's core differentiator is that it automatically applies professional-looking polish, including smooth cursor-tracking auto-zoom, cursor click highlighting and smoothing, customizable gradient or image backgrounds, inset shadows, and cinematic transitions, while the user is recording, rather than requiring manual keyframing in a traditional video editor. This 'beautiful screen recording' approach popularized a category now imitated by many competitors and is why Screen Studio is popular among product marketers, developer relations teams, indie founders, and educators who need to produce polished product demos, tutorials, and social clips quickly. In addition to screen capture, it supports simultaneous webcam recording, microphone and system audio capture, iOS device screen capture over USB, background noise removal and voice-level normalization, and locally processed transcript generation. Exports include up to 4K at 60fps, animated GIF, and shareable hosted links for quick distribution. Screen Studio uses a try-before-you-buy model: the app can be downloaded and used to record and edit videos for free, but exporting a finished video requires an active subscription. Pricing is billed either monthly, historically listed between roughly 20 and 29 USD per month depending on region and promotions, or annually, which works out to roughly 9 USD per month, 108 USD per year, when paid up front; the company also runs recurring promotions offering discounts on the annual plan and a student and education discount for verified academic email addresses. Screen Studio previously sold a one-time lifetime license for 229 USD, but that option has been discontinued for new customers and the product is now subscription-only, though existing lifetime license holders retain access to the app. Screen Studio is used internally by teams at companies such as Stripe, Vercel, Google, Dropbox, and Adobe for internal and external video content, and it competes directly with tools like Cap, ScreenFlow, Loom, and CleanShot X in the polished screen-recording space, though unlike ScreenFlow it does not offer a full multi-track non-linear editing timeline.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Screen Studio is a macOS screen recording app, launched in late 2022 by a small bootstrapped team led by Adam Pietrasiak in Krakow, Poland, built around the idea of automating the polish that used to require manual video editing.
It has become popular for product demos and tutorials thanks to automatic cursor-aware zoom and smooth animations applied during recording rather than in post-production.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Screen Studio automatically zooms and pans based on cursor activity, smooths and highlights cursor movement and clicks, and applies customizable backgrounds, shadows, and insets to give recordings a polished, presentation-ready look with minimal editing effort.

It also supports webcam, microphone, and system audio capture, iOS screen recording over USB, background noise removal, voice normalization, and locally generated transcripts, with export up to 4K at 60fps, GIF, and shareable hosted links.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The app can be downloaded and used to record and edit for free, but exporting requires a subscription. The annual plan is the best value at roughly 9 USD per month (108 USD per year), while monthly billing has historically run between about 20 and 29 USD per month. A previously offered one-time lifetime license for 229 USD is no longer available to new customers.

features.title: Automatic cursor-aware zoom and pan
features.description: Zooms and pans the recording automatically based on cursor movement and clicks, no manual keyframing needed.

features.description: Highlights and smooths cursor movement and click animations for a polished presentation feel.

features.title: Customizable backgrounds and shadows
features.description: Adds gradient or image backgrounds, inset spacing, and shadows around the recorded window.

features.title: Webcam, mic, and system audio capture
features.description: Records screen, webcam, microphone, and system audio simultaneously.

features.title: iOS device recording
features.description: Captures iOS device screens over a USB connection for mobile app demos.

features.title: Audio enhancement
features.description: Applies background noise removal and voice-level normalization automatically.

features.title: Local transcript generation
features.description: Generates video transcripts processed locally on the user's Mac.

features.title: High-quality export options
features.description: Exports up to 4K at 60fps, animated GIF, or a shareable hosted link.

pros: Produces polished demo videos with minimal manual editing
Simple, focused workflow purpose-built for product demos
High-quality 4K export and shareable link hosting
Used internally by well-known tech companies
Frequent promotions and a student discount lower the entry cost
cons: macOS only, no Windows or Linux version
No free export, a subscription is required to save finished videos
Lifetime license is no longer available to new customers
Lacks a full multi-track editing timeline found in ScreenFlow
Subscription cost can add up compared to one-time-purchase competitors

faqs.question: Is Screen Studio free?
faqs.answer: You can download Screen Studio and record and edit videos for free, but exporting a finished video requires an active paid subscription.

faqs.question: How much does Screen Studio cost?
faqs.answer: The annual plan works out to roughly 9 USD per month (108 USD per year), while monthly billing has historically run between about 20 and 29 USD per month.

faqs.question: Can I still buy a lifetime license?
faqs.answer: No, the one-time 229 USD lifetime license has been discontinued for new customers, though existing lifetime license holders keep their access.

faqs.question: Does Screen Studio work on Windows?
faqs.answer: No, Screen Studio is macOS only, requiring roughly macOS Ventura 13.1 or later.

faqs.question: Who built Screen Studio?
faqs.answer: Screen Studio was created by Polish developer Adam Pietrasiak along with co-founders Maciej and David, launching in late 2022 as a bootstrapped, indie-built product.

faqs.question: Does Screen Studio support webcam recording?
faqs.answer: Yes, it can record webcam, microphone, and system audio simultaneously alongside the screen capture.

faqs.question: Is there a student discount?
faqs.answer: Yes, Screen Studio has offered a discount for students and educators with a verified academic email address.

faqs.question: What is the maximum export quality?
faqs.answer: Screen Studio supports exporting video up to 4K resolution at 60 frames per second.

enrichment.short_description: Screen recording and video editing software for Mac from Telestream, combining capture with a full non-linear editing timeline for tutorials, demos, and courses.
enrichment.pricing_model: One-time purchase (perpetual license) with optional annual subscription add-ons
enrichment.seo_title: ScreenFlow Review 2026: Pricing and Features for Mac Video Editing
enrichment.seo_meta_description: ScreenFlow combines screen recording with a full video editing timeline for Mac. See its one-time pricing, features, and how it compares in 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: ScreenFlow is a screen recording and video editing application for macOS originally created by Vara Software and first released in 2008. Telestream, an American video technology company founded in 1998 and headquartered in Nevada City, California, acquired Vara Software in August 2008 and has developed ScreenFlow ever since alongside its broader lineup of video workflow products, including Wirecast. Telestream is a privately held company with roughly 445 to 500 employees globally as of 2026. Unlike lightweight screen recorders, ScreenFlow combines capture with a full multi-track, non-linear video editing timeline, letting users record their screen, webcam, microphone, and system audio simultaneously and then edit clips, add callouts, animated cursor effects, transitions, titles, and royalty-free music and stock media directly inside the same application, without needing to round-trip footage into a separate editor. It supports exporting to common formats including MP4, MOV, and animated GIF at up to 4K resolution, and direct publishing to YouTube, Vimeo, and other platforms. ScreenFlow is priced as a one-time perpetual-license purchase of 199 USD per copy rather than a recurring subscription, which distinguishes it from many modern screen recording competitors. Telestream also sells bundled options: the ScreenFlow Super Pak, around 275 USD, adds a year of access to a stock media library of music, sound effects, and video clips that renews at 99 USD per year, and the Super Pak Plus, around 325 USD, adds Premium Support with priority email response and a recurring upgrade discount. Because new major versions require a separate paid upgrade, ScreenFlow's effective long-term cost resembles a slower-cadence subscription for users who want to stay current with new macOS-compatible releases. ScreenFlow is popular among course creators, software trainers, IT documentation teams, and YouTubers who want an all-in-one Mac tool for both capturing and finishing tutorial-style video content, competing with Camtasia, which is cross-platform, Screen Studio, which is capture-focused without a full timeline, and OBS Studio, which is free but more technical. Volume and education discounts are available for multiple copies purchased together.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: ScreenFlow is Telestream's Mac-only screen recording and video editing application, originally built by Vara Software and first released in 2008 before Telestream acquired the product later that year.
It stands apart from single-purpose screen recorders by pairing capture with a full multi-track editing timeline, letting users finish a video entirely inside one application.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: ScreenFlow can simultaneously record the screen, webcam, microphone, and system audio, then lets users edit those tracks on a non-linear timeline with animated callouts, cursor effects, transitions, titles, and chroma key support.

It also includes access to royalty-free stock media, direct export to YouTube and Vimeo, and support for capturing iOS device screens, making it suited to producing finished tutorial and marketing videos rather than raw recordings.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The base ScreenFlow application is a one-time purchase priced at 199 USD, with no mandatory subscription. Telestream also sells bundles: the Super Pak (about 275 USD) adds a year of stock media access that renews at 99 USD per year, and the Super Pak Plus (about 325 USD) adds Premium Support for 59 USD per year on renewal.

features.description: Captures iOS device screens for mobile app tutorials and demos.

features.title: Chroma key support
features.description: Supports green screen background removal for webcam footage.

features.title: One-time perpetual license
features.description: Purchased once per major version rather than a mandatory recurring subscription.

faqs.question: Is ScreenFlow a one-time purchase or a subscription?
faqs.answer: ScreenFlow's base software is a one-time purchase priced at 199 USD; it is not a mandatory recurring subscription, though bundled stock media and support renew annually.

faqs.question: Does ScreenFlow work on Windows?
faqs.answer: No, ScreenFlow is Mac only.

faqs.question: Who makes ScreenFlow?
faqs.answer: ScreenFlow is developed by Telestream, an American video technology company founded in 1998 and headquartered in Nevada City, California; the product was originally created by Vara Software, acquired by Telestream in 2008.

faqs.question: Can ScreenFlow edit video, not just record it?
faqs.answer: Yes, it includes a full multi-track, non-linear editing timeline alongside its recording features.

faqs.question: Do new ScreenFlow versions cost extra?
faqs.answer: Yes, major version upgrades typically require a separate paid purchase rather than being free updates.

faqs.question: What resolution can ScreenFlow export?
faqs.answer: ScreenFlow supports export up to 4K resolution, along with MP4, MOV, and animated GIF formats.

faqs.question: What is the ScreenFlow Super Pak?
faqs.answer: It is a bundle, about 275 USD, that includes the base app plus a year of access to a stock media library that renews at 99 USD per year.

faqs.question: Does ScreenFlow support iOS screen recording?
faqs.answer: Yes, it can capture iOS device screens for mobile app demos and tutorials.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source desktop publishing software for professional page layout, typesetting, and print-ready PDF creation, comparable to Adobe InDesign.
enrichment.seo_title: Scribus Review 2026: Free Open-Source Desktop Publishing Software
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Scribus is a free, open-source alternative to InDesign for page layout and print design. See its features, current version, and who it fits best.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Scribus is a free and open-source desktop publishing application used for professional page layout, typesetting, and preparing files for commercial printing, often described as an open-source alternative to Adobe InDesign or QuarkXPress. The project was started in 2001 by German developer Franz Schmid, who began writing it to create simple print materials for his in-laws' hotel in Bavaria after finding no adequate free layout tools available for Linux; the first public release, version 0.3, shipped on July 9, 2001. Scribus is written in C++ using the Qt framework and is released under the GNU General Public License, and it is developed by a volunteer open-source community rather than a commercial company, meaning there is no headquarters, employee count, or formal customer support contract; support instead comes through community documentation, mailing lists, and forums. It runs natively on Linux, Windows, macOS, BSD, and Haiku. As of 2026 the current stable release series is 1.6.x, with 1.6.6 the latest maintenance release, following the major 1.6.0 release in January 2024 that added footnotes and endnotes, text variables, cross-references, vertical text justification, and orphan and widow control, plus a Weld feature for joining objects without grouping and a Symbol and Clone tool comparable to Adobe Illustrator's symbol system; a Picture Browser plugin for asset management ships with the app, and a newer 1.7.x development branch is in progress. Core capabilities include CMYK and spot color support, ICC color management for accurate print output, color separations for professional printing, versatile PDF export including PDF/X for prepress, master pages, text frames with advanced typography controls, vector drawing tools, and an extensive Python scripting API for automating repetitive layout tasks. Because it is free, Scribus is popular with independent publishers, nonprofits, small businesses, students, and hobbyists producing newsletters, brochures, magazines, books, and posters who want professional print-ready output without paying for a subscription-based design suite; it is less commonly adopted in large commercial publishing houses that rely on established ecosystems of plugins and collaborative workflow tools built around proprietary software.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Scribus is a free, open-source desktop publishing application started in 2001 by German developer Franz Schmid to fill a gap in Linux page-layout tools. It is developed by a volunteer community and released under the GNU General Public License.
It is commonly positioned as an open-source alternative to Adobe InDesign or QuarkXPress, built for professional print-ready layout rather than casual document creation.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Scribus supports CMYK and spot color, ICC color management, and color separations for professional prepress work, alongside versatile PDF export including PDF/X standards. The current 1.6.x stable series adds footnotes, endnotes, cross-references, text variables, and orphan and widow control.
It also includes a Weld tool for joining objects without grouping, a Symbol and Clone facility, master pages, vector drawing tools, and a Python scripting API for automating repetitive layout tasks.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Scribus is entirely free and open source with no paid tiers, license fees, or subscriptions. It is funded and maintained through volunteer contributions rather than commercial sales, and runs on Linux, Windows, macOS, BSD, and Haiku.

features.title: Professional page layout and typesetting

features.description: Exports versatile PDFs including PDF/X standards suited for prepress workflows.

features.description: Supports footnotes, endnotes, cross-references, and text variables added in version 1.6.

features.title: Vector drawing and Symbol/Clone tools
features.description: Includes vector drawing tools and an Illustrator-style Symbol and Clone system.

features.title: Python scripting API
features.description: Automates repetitive layout tasks through an extensive Python scripting interface.

features.description: Supports reusable master page templates for consistent multi-page documents.

features.description: Runs natively on Linux, Windows, macOS, BSD, and Haiku.

pros: Completely free with no subscription or license fees
Professional-grade print and prepress color management
Active open-source community with regular updates
Cross-platform, including native Linux support, which is rare among DTP tools
Extensible via a Python scripting API
cons: Steeper learning curve and less polished UI than commercial alternatives
Smaller plugin and extension ecosystem than proprietary competitors
No official commercial support or service-level agreement
Limited file interchange with proprietary InDesign or QuarkXPress files
Slower development pace given its volunteer-driven model

faqs.question: Is Scribus really free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Scribus is free and open-source software released under the GNU General Public License, with no paid tiers or subscriptions.

faqs.question: Who develops Scribus?
faqs.answer: Scribus is developed by a volunteer open-source community; it was originally started in 2001 by German developer Franz Schmid.

faqs.question: What operating systems does Scribus support?
faqs.answer: Scribus runs natively on Linux, Windows, macOS, BSD, and Haiku.

faqs.question: Is Scribus a good alternative to Adobe InDesign?
faqs.answer: Scribus offers many professional print-layout features comparable to InDesign, including CMYK, spot color, and PDF/X export, though it has a smaller ecosystem and steeper learning curve.

faqs.question: Can Scribus export print-ready PDFs?
faqs.answer: Yes, Scribus supports versatile PDF export including PDF/X standards designed for professional prepress printing.

faqs.question: Does Scribus support scripting or automation?
faqs.answer: Yes, Scribus includes an extensive Python scripting API for automating layout tasks.

faqs.question: What is the current version of Scribus?
faqs.answer: As of 2026 the current stable release series is 1.6.x, with 1.6.6 the latest maintenance release, while a 1.7.x development branch is in progress.

faqs.question: Can Scribus open InDesign or QuarkXPress files?
faqs.answer: Scribus has limited support for interchange with proprietary formats like InDesign or QuarkXPress files, and full fidelity is not guaranteed.

enrichment.short_description: Visual thinking and note-taking tool that combines infinite canvases with connected notes and cards for research, brainstorming, and knowledge mapping.
enrichment.pricing_model: Freemium (free plan plus paid subscription tiers)
enrichment.starting_price: 5 USD per month billed annually (Personal plan)
enrichment.seo_title: Scrintal Review 2026: Pricing and Features for Visual Note-Taking
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Scrintal combines infinite canvases with linked notes for visual thinking and research. See its pricing plans, features, and best use cases in 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Scrintal is a visual thinking and note-taking application that lets users combine infinite whiteboard-style canvases with linked notes, called cards, to brainstorm, research, and organize ideas visually rather than in a purely linear document. The company was founded in 2020 by Ece Kural and Furkan Bayraktar and is headquartered in Stockholm, Sweden; in January 2023 Scrintal raised a 1 million EUR seed round led by Spintop Ventures with participation from existing investor Icebreaker VC, funding continued development of its visual canvas and collaboration features. The core product lets users create cards containing rich text, images, and embeds, arrange them freely on a zoomable canvas, and draw connections between related cards to build a visual map of their thinking, positioning it as a hybrid between a mind-mapping tool, a whiteboard, and a note-taking app like Notion or Obsidian. Scrintal supports real-time multi-user collaboration on shared boards, making it usable by teams doing collaborative research, content planning, or product strategy work, not just individual note-takers. Pricing follows a freemium model: the free plan includes unlimited mind maps and boards with a cap of around 100 cards per board and 100MB of storage, suitable for individual light use. Paid tiers remove these caps: a Personal plan is priced around 5 USD per month, billed annually, with unlimited cards per board and 1GB of storage, while the Scrintal Pro plan is priced around 9 USD per month, billed annually, and adds unlimited file uploads, real-time collaboration features, priority support, and access to community calls with the Scrintal team. Because pricing is billed annually for the lowest per-month rate, monthly billing where offered costs more per month than the annual commitment. Scrintal is used by researchers, writers, students, UX designers, and product teams who think spatially and want to visually cluster related ideas, references, and sources rather than scrolling through a long linear document; it competes with tools like Heptabase, Milanote, Miro, and Obsidian Canvas in the visual knowledge-management space.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Scrintal is a Stockholm-based visual note-taking tool, founded in 2020 by Ece Kural and Furkan Bayraktar, that blends an infinite canvas with linked note cards so users can think and organize ideas spatially.

The company raised a 1 million EUR seed round in January 2023 led by Spintop Ventures, with participation from Icebreaker VC, to fund continued development of its visual canvas and collaboration features.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Users create cards containing rich text, images, and embeds, then arrange and connect them freely on a zoomable canvas to visually map relationships between ideas, sources, and references.
Scrintal supports real-time multi-user collaboration on shared boards, making it suitable for team research, content planning, and product strategy work in addition to individual note-taking.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: A free plan offers unlimited boards capped at about 100 cards per board and 100MB of storage. The Personal plan, around 5 USD per month billed annually, removes the card cap and adds 1GB of storage, while Scrintal Pro, around 9 USD per month billed annually, adds unlimited file uploads, real-time collaboration, and priority support.

features.description: Draws explicit relationship lines between related cards to map out thinking.

features.description: Removes free-plan caps on cards per board and file uploads.

features.title: Cross-device sync
features.description: Keeps boards synced across devices for continued access.

features.title: Community calls and priority support
features.description: Pro plan subscribers get access to team community calls and priority support.

features.title: File uploads and attachments
features.description: Supports uploading files and images directly into cards, with storage limits by plan.

pros: Intuitive visual and spatial approach to note-taking
Real-time collaboration on shared boards
Generous free plan for individual light use
Backed by seed funding and under active development
Well suited to research and idea-mapping workflows
cons: Free plan capped at around 100 cards per board
Smaller ecosystem and fewer integrations than Notion or Obsidian
Can become visually cluttered on very large boards
Primarily web-based, dependent on an internet connection
Higher effective monthly cost if billed monthly instead of annually

faqs.question: Is Scrintal free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Scrintal has a free plan with unlimited boards capped at about 100 cards per board and 100MB of storage.

faqs.question: How much does Scrintal Pro cost?
faqs.answer: Scrintal Pro is priced around 9 USD per month when billed annually.

faqs.question: Who founded Scrintal?
faqs.answer: Scrintal was founded in 2020 by Ece Kural and Furkan Bayraktar and is headquartered in Stockholm, Sweden.

faqs.question: Does Scrintal support team collaboration?
faqs.answer: Yes, Scrintal supports real-time multi-user collaboration on shared canvases.

faqs.question: How is Scrintal different from Notion?
faqs.answer: Scrintal centers on a visual, spatial canvas with connected cards, while Notion is primarily a linear document and database tool.

faqs.question: Has Scrintal raised funding?
faqs.answer: Yes, Scrintal raised a 1 million EUR seed round in January 2023 led by Spintop Ventures with participation from Icebreaker VC.

faqs.question: What is the difference between the Personal and Pro plans?
faqs.answer: The Personal plan removes the free plan's card cap and adds 1GB of storage, while Pro adds unlimited file uploads, real-time collaboration, and priority support.

faqs.question: Is Scrintal available on mobile?
faqs.answer: Scrintal is primarily a web-based application accessed through a browser across devices.

enrichment.short_description: European e-signature and digital agreement platform offering electronic signing, identity verification through eID, and web forms for businesses.
enrichment.seo_title: Scrive Review 2026: Pricing and Features for European E-Signatures
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Scrive is a Stockholm-based e-signature and eID verification platform for European businesses. See its features, pricing model, and best use cases.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Scrive is a European electronic signature and digital agreement platform founded in Stockholm, Sweden in 2010 by two university friends who initially built the product to simplify signing agreements for international performers touring in Sweden. The company remains headquartered in Stockholm and is backed by growth equity firm Vitruvian Partners; employee counts reported across business databases range from roughly 150 to about 245 people as of late 2025 and 2026. Scrive states it is trusted by more than 13,000 companies across over 60 countries and processes more than 100 million transactions per year, with a strong focus on the European market, including deep integration with Nordic and EU electronic identification schemes such as BankID in Sweden and Norway, MitID in Denmark, and other national eID providers, which differentiates it from more US-centric e-signature tools for organizations that need strong identity verification alongside signatures. The product suite includes eSign Online, a self-service signing portal, eSign GO and eSign API for embedding signing into internal workflows and software, an eID Hub and ID Check for identity verification and authentication, and Forms Builder and eSign Forms for combining data collection with signature capture in a single flow. Scrive does not publish standard self-serve pricing tiers on its website; pricing pages route users to per-product quote requests or a sales conversation, and one publicly visible institutional example, a Swedish university's staff portal, showed a rate of roughly SEK 300 per month per user including 50 signing transactions, with additional transactions billed at about SEK 2 each, though this reflects a negotiated institutional rate rather than list pricing available to all customers. Scrive is commonly adopted by banks, telecom companies, HR and staffing firms, real estate businesses, and public sector organizations across Europe that need legally binding, audit-trailed digital agreements combined with strong identity assurance for regulatory compliance such as GDPR and eIDAS. It competes with DocuSign, Adobe Acrobat Sign, Dropbox Sign, and other eIDAS-compliant regional providers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Scrive is a Stockholm-headquartered e-signature and digital agreement platform founded in 2010, focused primarily on the European market and deeply integrated with Nordic and EU electronic identification systems.
The company is backed by growth equity firm Vitruvian Partners and reports serving more than 13,000 companies across over 60 countries, processing more than 100 million transactions per year.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Scrive's product suite spans a self-service signing portal (eSign Online), API and embedded signing tools (eSign API, eSign GO), and identity verification services (eID Hub, ID Check) that connect to national eID schemes such as BankID and MitID.

It also offers Forms Builder and eSign Forms for combining data collection with e-signature capture in a single flow, along with full audit trails needed for regulatory compliance such as GDPR and eIDAS.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Scrive does not publish self-serve pricing; each product line, from eSign Online to the API and eID services, routes to a custom quote after a sales conversation. Publicly visible institutional pricing has shown rates around SEK 300 per month per user including 50 transactions, with overage billed per additional transaction.

features.description: Offers an outsourced, managed signing workflow option for businesses that want less setup work.

Combines e-signature and identity verification in one platform
High transaction scale reported, over 100 million per year
Flexible deployment through portal, API, or managed service
Established company with over a decade in the market
cons: No public self-serve pricing, requires contacting sales
Primarily optimized for European and Nordic markets
Onboarding and API integration can require technical work
Per-transaction cost model can be harder to predict at scale
Smaller brand recognition outside Europe compared to global competitors

faqs.question: How much does Scrive cost?
faqs.answer: Scrive does not publish standard pricing; costs are quote-based after contacting sales, and can include per-transaction and subscription components.

faqs.question: Where is Scrive based?
faqs.answer: Scrive is headquartered in Stockholm, Sweden, and was founded in 2010.

faqs.question: Does Scrive support identity verification, not just signatures?
faqs.answer: Yes, its eID Hub and ID Check products integrate with national electronic identification schemes such as BankID and MitID to verify signer identity.

faqs.question: Is Scrive legally binding across Europe?
faqs.answer: Yes, Scrive's e-signatures are designed to be eIDAS-compliant and legally recognized across the European Union.

faqs.question: Can Scrive be embedded into other software?
faqs.answer: Yes, the eSign API allows businesses to embed signing directly into their own applications and workflows.

faqs.question: How many companies use Scrive?
faqs.answer: Scrive states it is used by more than 13,000 companies across over 60 countries.

faqs.question: Does Scrive offer a managed signing service?
faqs.answer: Yes, eSign GO offers an outsourced, managed version of the signing workflow.

faqs.question: What industries commonly use Scrive?
faqs.answer: Banks, telecom companies, HR and staffing firms, real estate businesses, and public sector organizations across Europe are common Scrive users.

enrichment.short_description: Cloud-based governance, risk, and compliance (GRC) automation platform that helps growth-stage and cloud-native companies manage security compliance, risk assessments, and audits.
enrichment.starting_price: Custom pricing; publicly reported ranges roughly 15,000 to 20,000 USD or more per year for early-stage companies
enrichment.headquarters: Bengaluru, India (with a US office in the Palo Alto and Milpitas, California area)
enrichment.seo_title: Scrut Automation Review 2026: Pricing and Features for GRC Compliance
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Scrut Automation automates SOC 2, ISO 27001, and other compliance frameworks for cloud-native companies. See its features, pricing ranges, and use cases.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Scrut Automation is a cloud-based governance, risk, and compliance platform that helps growth-stage and cloud-native companies automate security compliance work such as SOC 2, ISO 27001, GDPR, and HIPAA framework readiness, continuous control monitoring, risk assessments, and audit preparation. The company was founded in August 2021 by Aayush Ghosh Choudhury as CEO, Jayesh Gadewar as CTO, and Kush Kaushik, reportedly after the founders built internal compliance automation tooling at a previous procurement SaaS startup to satisfy enterprise buyers demanding SOC 2 certification, later spinning that tooling into a standalone product. Scrut is headquartered in Bengaluru, India, with a US presence in the Palo Alto and Milpitas, California area, and employee counts across business databases range from roughly 125 to 185 people as of 2025 and 2026. The company has raised about 20.5 million USD in total funding, including a 10 million USD growth round in 2024 backed by investors such as Lightspeed India, MassMutual Ventures, Endiya Partners, and Crane Venture Partners. The platform automates evidence collection by connecting to cloud infrastructure such as AWS, Azure, and GCP, along with HR, identity, and ticketing systems, to continuously monitor security controls rather than relying on manual spreadsheet-based audit prep. It includes a risk register, vendor and third-party risk management, policy management, and a marketplace of compliance frameworks that customers can map to a single set of underlying controls to avoid duplicating work across multiple certifications, an approach some third-party reviews describe as avoiding a 'framework tax.' Scrut does not publish self-serve pricing on its website; pricing is quote-based following a sales conversation, and third-party industry estimates place typical annual contract values around 15,000 to 20,000 USD per year for early-stage companies under 50 employees, 18,000 to 30,000 USD for mid-size SaaS companies over 50 employees, and 40,000 USD or more for enterprise customers needing five or more compliance frameworks, sometimes alongside a one-time onboarding fee of 1,000 to 5,000 USD that may be waived on larger contracts. Scrut competes with Vanta, Drata, Secureframe, and Sprinto in the compliance automation space, primarily targeting startups and mid-market SaaS companies going through their first or subsequent security certifications.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Scrut Automation is a Bengaluru, India-headquartered GRC platform, founded in August 2021 by Aayush Ghosh Choudhury, Jayesh Gadewar, and Kush Kaushik, that automates security compliance work for growth-stage and cloud-native companies.
The company has raised about 20.5 million USD in total funding and maintains a US office in the Palo Alto and Milpitas, California area alongside its India headquarters.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Scrut connects to cloud infrastructure such as AWS, Azure, and GCP, along with HR, identity, and ticketing systems, to continuously monitor security controls and automate evidence collection for audits rather than relying on manual spreadsheets.

The platform includes a risk register, vendor and third-party risk management, policy management, and a framework marketplace that maps shared controls across multiple certifications such as SOC 2, ISO 27001, GDPR, and HIPAA to reduce duplicate audit work.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Scrut does not publish self-serve pricing; costs are quote-based after a sales conversation. Third-party industry estimates suggest annual contracts of roughly 15,000 to 20,000 USD for early-stage companies, 18,000 to 30,000 USD for mid-size SaaS companies, and 40,000 USD or more for enterprise customers needing multiple frameworks, sometimes with a separate onboarding fee.

features.description: Monitors and assesses the security posture of vendors and third parties.

features.description: Manages and tracks employee acknowledgment of security policies and training.

features.title: Audit management workspace
features.description: Provides a collaboration space for working with external auditors.

features.description: Offers a public-facing trust or security page to share compliance posture with prospects and customers.

Supports mapping controls across multiple frameworks at once
Backed by significant venture funding and active development
Includes vendor risk and policy management beyond certification tracking
Positioned as relatively affordable for early-stage companies versus some larger competitors
cons: No public self-serve pricing, requires a sales conversation
Can represent a significant annual cost for early-stage startups
A separate onboarding fee may apply on top of the subscription
Smaller brand recognition than category leaders like Vanta or Drata
Best suited to cloud-native companies, less tailored to on-premise or legacy environments

faqs.question: How much does Scrut Automation cost?
faqs.answer: Scrut does not publish public pricing; third-party estimates suggest roughly 15,000 to 20,000 USD per year for early-stage companies, scaling up to 40,000 USD or more for enterprise customers with multiple frameworks.

faqs.question: Who founded Scrut Automation?
faqs.answer: Scrut was founded in August 2021 by Aayush Ghosh Choudhury, Jayesh Gadewar, and Kush Kaushik.

faqs.question: Where is Scrut Automation headquartered?
faqs.answer: Scrut is headquartered in Bengaluru, India, with a US office in the Palo Alto and Milpitas, California area.

faqs.question: What compliance frameworks does Scrut support?
faqs.answer: Scrut supports frameworks including SOC 2, ISO 27001, GDPR, and HIPAA, mapping shared controls across multiple frameworks at once.

faqs.question: Does Scrut integrate with cloud infrastructure?
faqs.answer: Yes, it connects to AWS, Azure, and GCP, along with HR, identity, and ticketing systems, to automate evidence collection.

faqs.question: How much funding has Scrut raised?
faqs.answer: Scrut has raised about 20.5 million USD in total funding, including a 10 million USD growth round in 2024.

faqs.question: Who are Scrut's main competitors?
faqs.answer: Scrut competes with Vanta, Drata, Secureframe, and Sprinto in the compliance automation market.

faqs.question: Does Scrut charge an onboarding fee?
faqs.answer: Third-party estimates suggest a one-time onboarding fee of roughly 1,000 to 5,000 USD may apply, though it can be waived on larger contracts.
